--      has billions of rows and the customer × ym grid is small.
--   2. Date-range WHERE for partition pruning. The classifier needs
--      one extra month before the report window so prev_m / next_m
--      LAG/LEAD aren't NULL inside the report range.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WITH user_activity AS (
    SELECT customer_id, strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) AS ym
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= '2024-12-01'  -- one month buffer for LAG/LEAD
      AND order_date <  '2025-05-01'
    GROUP BY customer_id, strftime('%Y-%m', order_date)
),
edges AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        ym,
        date(ym || '-01') AS current_m,
        LAG (date(ym || '-01')) OVER w AS prev_m,
        LEAD(date(ym || '-01')) OVER w AS next_m
    FROM user_activity
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY ym)
),
classifications AS (
    SELECT
        ym, customer_id,
        CASE
            WHEN prev_m IS NULL                       THEN 'new'
            WHEN prev_m = date(current_m, '-1 month') THEN 'existing'
            ELSE                                            'resurrected'
        END AS bucket,
        CASE
            WHEN next_m IS NULL OR next_m > date(current_m, '+1 month')
            THEN strftime('%Y-%m', date(current_m, '+1 month'))
        END AS churn_ym
    FROM edges
)
SELECT ym AS year_month, bucket, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers
FROM (
    SELECT ym, customer_id, bucket FROM classifications
    UNION ALL
    SELECT churn_ym, customer_id, 'churned'
    FROM classifications WHERE churn_ym IS NOT NULL
)
GROUP BY ym, bucket
ORDER BY ym,
    CASE bucket WHEN 'new' THEN 1 WHEN 'resurrected' THEN 2
                WHEN 'existing' THEN 3 ELSE 4 END;

questions.prompt:
Resurrected Customers — Cycle Count, AOV Uplift, Y1 vs Y2 Resurrection Rate

Snapshot 2025-04-01. Use the gaps-and-islands pattern: assign each order an island_id that bumps every time the previous-order gap exceeds 90 days. For each customer with at least 2 islands (i.e. at least 1 resurrection cycle), return:
  customer_id
  total_islands            — number of distinct active phases
  resurrection_cycles      — total_islands - 1
  avg_gap_days             — average idle gap between islands
  max_gap_days             — longest idle gap
  avg_pre_churn_aov        — AOV of last order in each closing island
  avg_comeback_aov         — AOV of first order in each new island
  aov_uplift               — comeback - pre_churn (positive = healthy)
  y1_resurrections         — comeback events in the rolling-12 window
  y2_resurrections         — comeback events in the prior-12 window
  resurrection_trend       — 'rising','falling','stable','y1_only'
  most_recent_comeback     — date

Sort by resurrection_cycles DESC, aov_uplift DESC.

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), params(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date DATE NOT NULL,
    amount NUMERIC NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1,1,'2023-04-10',100),(2,1,'2023-04-25',120),
  (3,1,'2023-08-27',180),(4,1,'2023-09-10',210),
  (5,1,'2023-12-24',260),(6,1,'2024-01-07',290),
  (7,1,'2024-07-05',340),(8,1,'2024-08-20',380),(9,1,'2025-01-12',410),
  (10,2,'2023-02-15',60),(11,2,'2023-04-22',70),
  (12,2,'2023-08-30',95),(13,2,'2023-12-15',105),(14,2,'2024-02-20',95),
  (15,2,'2024-09-15',88),
  (16,3,'2024-04-04',300),(17,3,'2024-04-18',280),
  (18,3,'2024-07-27',260),
  (19,3,'2024-11-04',180),
  (20,3,'2025-02-22',150),
  (21,4,'2024-01-05',100),(22,4,'2024-02-15',100),
  (23,4,'2024-04-10',100),(24,4,'2024-06-22',100),(25,4,'2024-09-08',100),
  (26,4,'2024-11-22',100),(27,4,'2025-02-15',100);

WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-04-01' AS snapshot),
flagged AS (
    SELECT o.customer_id, o.order_date, o.amount,
        CASE
          WHEN LAG(o.order_date) OVER w IS NULL THEN 1
          WHEN (o.order_date - LAG(o.order_date) OVER w) > 90 THEN 1
          ELSE 0
        END AS opens_island,
        ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - o.order_date) AS days_back
    FROM orders o
    WHERE o.order_date >= (SELECT snapshot FROM params) - INTERVAL '730 days'
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY o.order_date)
),
with_island AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount, days_back, opens_island,
        SUM(opens_island) OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ) AS island_id
    FROM flagged
),
windowed AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount, days_back, island_id,
        FIRST_VALUE(amount) OVER w_island AS first_amount,
        LAST_VALUE(amount)  OVER w_island AS last_amount
    FROM with_island
    WINDOW w_island AS (
        PARTITION BY customer_id, island_id
        ORDER BY order_date
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
    )
),
islands AS (
    SELECT customer_id, island_id,
        MIN(order_date) AS island_start, MAX(order_date) AS island_end,
        MAX(first_amount) AS first_amount, MAX(last_amount) AS last_amount
    FROM windowed
    GROUP BY customer_id, island_id
),
phase_pairs AS (
    SELECT customer_id, island_id,
        island_start AS comeback_date,
        first_amount AS comeback_aov,
        LAG(island_end)  OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY island_id) AS prev_island_end,
        LAG(last_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY island_id) AS prev_island_aov,
        ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - island_start) AS days_back_comeback
    FROM islands
),
resurrections AS (
    SELECT customer_id, comeback_date, comeback_aov,
        prev_island_aov AS pre_churn_aov,
        (comeback_date - prev_island_end) AS gap_days,
        days_back_comeback
    FROM phase_pairs
    WHERE prev_island_end IS NOT NULL
      AND (comeback_date - prev_island_end) > 90
),
total_islands_per_cust AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MAX(island_id) AS total_islands
    FROM islands GROUP BY customer_id
),
per_customer AS (
    SELECT r.customer_id, ti.total_islands,
        COUNT(*) AS resurrection_cycles,
        ROUND(AVG(gap_days), 0) AS avg_gap_days,
        MAX(gap_days) AS max_gap_days,
        ROUND(AVG(pre_churn_aov), 2) AS avg_pre_churn_aov,
        ROUND(AVG(comeback_aov), 2) AS avg_comeback_aov,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back_comeback < 365 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS y1_resurrections,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back_comeback >= 365 AND days_back_comeback < 730 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS y2_resurrections,
        MAX(comeback_date) AS most_recent_comeback
    FROM resurrections r
    JOIN total_islands_per_cust ti ON ti.customer_id = r.customer_id
    GROUP BY r.customer_id, ti.total_islands
)
SELECT customer_id, total_islands, resurrection_cycles,
    avg_gap_days, max_gap_days, avg_pre_churn_aov, avg_comeback_aov,
    ROUND(avg_comeback_aov - avg_pre_churn_aov, 2) AS aov_uplift,
    y1_resurrections, y2_resurrections,
    CASE
        WHEN y2_resurrections = 0 AND y1_resurrections > 0 THEN 'y1_only'
        WHEN y1_resurrections > y2_resurrections THEN 'rising'
        WHEN y1_resurrections < y2_resurrections THEN 'falling'
        ELSE 'stable'
    END AS resurrection_trend,
    most_recent_comeback
FROM per_customer
ORDER BY resurrection_cycles DESC, aov_uplift DESC;

questions.prompt:
Return-to-Purchase Fraud — L7 / L28 / L90 Trending Detector

Snapshot 2025-04-01. Per customer, return:
  customer_id, lifetime_orders, lifetime_returns, lifetime_return_rate,
  l7_orders, l7_returns, l7_return_rate,
  l28_orders, l28_returns, l28_return_rate,
  l90_orders, l90_returns, l90_return_rate,
  max_7d_return_burst    — most returns inside any 7-day rolling window,
  fraud_flag             — 'low_volume' if L90 orders < 5,
                           'burst' if max_7d_return_burst > 4,
                           'high_return_rate' if L28 rate > 0.40,
                           'rising_returns' if L28 rate > 1.5 × L90 rate,
                           else 'clean'.

Sort by fraud_flag priority (burst, high_return_rate, rising_returns,
low_volume, clean), then by l28_return_rate DESC.

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status, l28_orders, l7_orders, l90_orders), returns(return_id, order_id, return_date, refund_amount, reason, customer_id, l28_returns, l7_returns, l90_returns), params(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status, l28_orders, l7_orders, l90_orders), return_pairs(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status, l28_orders, l7_orders, l90_orders)
```
questions.model_answer:
WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-04-01' AS snapshot),
o_e AS (
    SELECT o.*, ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - o.order_date::date) AS days_back
    FROM orders o
),
r_e AS (
    SELECT r.return_id, o.customer_id, r.return_date,
           ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - r.return_date::date) AS days_back
    FROM returns r
    JOIN orders o ON r.order_id = o.order_id
),
return_pairs AS (
    SELECT a.customer_id, a.return_date AS anchor,
           COUNT(*) AS burst_count
    FROM r_e a
    JOIN r_e b ON b.customer_id = a.customer_id
              AND b.return_date >= a.return_date
              AND (b.return_date::date - a.return_date::date) < 7
    GROUP BY a.customer_id, a.return_date
),
burst AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MAX(burst_count) AS max_7d_return_burst
    FROM return_pairs GROUP BY customer_id
),
order_w AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS lifetime_orders,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_orders,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_orders,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_orders
    FROM o_e GROUP BY customer_id
),
return_w AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS lifetime_returns,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_returns,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_returns,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_returns
    FROM r_e GROUP BY customer_id
),
joined AS (
    SELECT o.customer_id,
        o.lifetime_orders, COALESCE(r.lifetime_returns, 0) AS lifetime_returns,
        o.l7_orders, COALESCE(r.l7_returns, 0) AS l7_returns,
        o.l28_orders, COALESCE(r.l28_returns, 0) AS l28_returns,
        o.l90_orders, COALESCE(r.l90_returns, 0) AS l90_returns,
        COALESCE(b.max_7d_return_burst, 0) AS max_7d_return_burst
    FROM order_w o
    LEFT JOIN return_w r ON r.customer_id = o.customer_id
    LEFT JOIN burst b ON b.customer_id = o.customer_id
)
SELECT customer_id, lifetime_orders, lifetime_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*lifetime_returns/NULLIF(lifetime_orders,0), 2) AS lifetime_return_rate,
    l7_orders, l7_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*l7_returns/NULLIF(l7_orders,0), 2) AS l7_return_rate,
    l28_orders, l28_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0), 2) AS l28_return_rate,
    l90_orders, l90_returns,
    ROUND(1.0*l90_returns/NULLIF(l90_orders,0), 2) AS l90_return_rate,
    max_7d_return_burst,
    CASE
        WHEN l90_orders < 5 THEN 'low_volume'
        WHEN max_7d_return_burst > 4 THEN 'burst'
        WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0) > 0.40 THEN 'high_return_rate'
        WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0)
             > 1.5*COALESCE(1.0*l90_returns/NULLIF(l90_orders,0), 0)
             AND l28_returns >= 2 THEN 'rising_returns'
        ELSE 'clean'
    END AS fraud_flag
FROM joined
ORDER BY
    CASE
      WHEN max_7d_return_burst > 4 THEN 1
      WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0) > 0.40 THEN 2
      WHEN 1.0*l28_returns/NULLIF(l28_orders,0)
           > 1.5*COALESCE(1.0*l90_returns/NULLIF(l90_orders,0),0)
           AND l28_returns >= 2 THEN 3
      WHEN l90_orders < 5 THEN 4
      ELSE 5
    END,
    l28_return_rate DESC;

questions.prompt:
Fraud Signal — More Than 3 Shipping Addresses In a 30-Day Window

Return one row per offending customer:
  customer_id | window_start | window_end | distinct_addresses | total_amount

where the window is the first 30-day stretch in which the customer's distinct shipping-address count exceeds 3 (i.e. ≥ 4 distinct addresses). Order by distinct_addresses DESC, then by customer_id ASC.

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, customer_id, order_ts, amount, shipping_address, order_date, status)
```
questions.model_answer:
-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Setup — TIMESTAMP-typed order_ts so the INTERVAL window  │
-- │  arithmetic runs natively in PostgreSQL.                  │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER,
    order_ts TIMESTAMP, amount NUMERIC, shipping_address TEXT
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- Customer 1: 4 different addresses, all within 30 days → fraud signal
  (1, 1, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 200, '1 Pine St'),
  (2, 1, '2025-04-05 12:30:00', 350, '7 Birch Ln'),
  (3, 1, '2025-04-12 18:45:00', 180, '22 Cedar Rd'),
  (4, 1, '2025-04-19 02:55:00', 410, '88 Elm Way'),
  -- Customer 2: 1 address only — not flagged
  (5, 2, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 150, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (6, 2, '2025-04-05 14:00:00', 180, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (7, 2, '2025-04-10 19:00:00', 220, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (8, 2, '2025-04-15 09:00:00', 100, '12 Oak Dr'),
  (9, 2, '2025-04-20 09:00:00', 100, '12 Oak Dr'),
  -- Customer 3: 4 addresses but spread over 5 months — not flagged
  (10, 3, '2025-01-01 09:00:00', 120, '5 Maple Ct'),
  (11, 3, '2025-02-15 14:00:00', 130, '9 Walnut Ave'),
  (12, 3, '2025-04-04 19:00:00', 140, '11 Spruce Pl'),
  (13, 3, '2025-05-30 19:00:00', 150, '13 Aspen Way'),
  -- Customer 4: 3 addresses in 25 days — at threshold, just under the > 3 rule
  (14, 4, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 90, 'A1'),
  (15, 4, '2025-04-10 18:00:00', 90, 'A2'),
  (16, 4, '2025-04-25 08:00:00', 90, 'A3'),
  -- Customer 5: 5 addresses in 25 days → fraud signal
  (17, 5, '2025-03-05 09:00:00', 50, 'X1'),
  (18, 5, '2025-03-10 09:00:00', 50, 'X2'),
  (19, 5, '2025-03-15 09:00:00', 50, 'X3'),
  (20, 5, '2025-03-20 09:00:00', 50, 'X4'),
  (21, 5, '2025-03-30 09:00:00', 50, 'X5');

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Solution — collapse to (customer, address) first-seen,   │
-- │  then self-join for 30-day windows. Native PG INTERVAL    │
-- │  replaces SQLite's datetime(ts, '+30 days').              │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH first_addr AS (
    SELECT customer_id, shipping_address,
           MIN(order_ts) AS first_ts,
           SUM(amount)   AS addr_spend
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY customer_id, shipping_address
),
windowed AS (
    SELECT a.customer_id,
           a.first_ts                 AS window_start,
           MAX(b.first_ts)            AS window_end,
           COUNT(*)                   AS distinct_addresses,
           SUM(b.addr_spend)          AS total_amount
    FROM first_addr a
    JOIN first_addr b
      ON b.customer_id = a.customer_id
     AND b.first_ts BETWEEN a.first_ts
                        AND a.first_ts + INTERVAL '30 days'
    GROUP BY a.customer_id, a.first_ts
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT windowed.*,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id
                              ORDER BY distinct_addresses DESC,
                                       window_start ASC) AS rn
    FROM windowed
)
SELECT customer_id, window_start, window_end,
       distinct_addresses, total_amount
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY distinct_addresses DESC, customer_id ASC;

questions.prompt:
Passive Customer Engagement Velocity — L7 vs L28 Daily Rate

Snapshot 2025-04-01. For each customer with at least 5 lifetime sessions and ZERO orders, return:
  customer_id, lifetime_sessions, lifetime_cart_adds,
  l7_sessions, l28_sessions, l90_sessions,
  l7_cart_adds, l28_cart_adds,
  l7_daily_sessions, l28_daily_sessions,    -- per-day rates
  cart_velocity   = l7_daily_sessions / NULLIF(l28_daily_sessions, 0),
  engagement_trend ∈ {'warming','cooling','stable','cold'}

  • 'cold'    if l28_sessions = 0
  • 'warming' if l7_daily_sessions > 1.5 × l28_daily_sessions
  • 'cooling' if l7_daily_sessions < 0.5 × l28_daily_sessions
  • else 'stable'

Sort by 'warming' first, then by l28_sessions DESC.

Schema:
```
sessions(session_id, customer_id, session_date, added_to_cart, checked_out), orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), params(customer_id, signup_date)
```
questions.model_answer:
WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-04-01' AS snapshot),
sess AS (
    SELECT s.session_id, s.customer_id, s.session_date,
           CASE WHEN s.added_to_cart::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN s.added_to_cart::numeric ELSE 0 END AS added_to_cart,
           ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - s.session_date::date) AS days_back
    FROM sessions s
)
SELECT s.customer_id,
    COUNT(*) AS lifetime_sessions,
    SUM(s.added_to_cart) AS lifetime_cart_adds,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_sessions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_sessions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_sessions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN s.added_to_cart ELSE 0 END) AS l7_cart_adds,
    SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN s.added_to_cart ELSE 0 END) AS l28_cart_adds,
    ROUND(1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 7.0, 2) AS l7_daily_sessions,
    ROUND(1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 28.0, 2) AS l28_daily_sessions,
    ROUND(
        (1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 7.0)
        / NULLIF(1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / 28.0, 0),
    2) AS cart_velocity,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0 THEN 'cold'
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           > 1.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 'warming'
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           < 0.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 'cooling'
        ELSE 'stable'
    END AS engagement_trend
FROM sess s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = s.customer_id)
GROUP BY s.customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
ORDER BY
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0 THEN 4
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           > 1.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 1
        WHEN (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/7.0)
           < 0.5 * (1.0*SUM(CASE WHEN days_back<28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/28.0) THEN 3
        ELSE 2
    END,
    l28_sessions DESC;

questions.prompt:
Fraud-Aware Incentive Eligibility — Severity-Weighted L7/L28/L90 Risk Score

Given the campaign with name = 'win_back_q2_2025' and snapshot 2025-05-01, return:
  customer_id, tier, lifecycle_state,
  l7_signals, l28_signals, l90_signals,
  high_l90, medium_l30, low_l28,
  longest_burst   — longest run of consecutive calendar days
                    with at least one signal (gaps-and-islands).
  risk_score   = (HIGH within 90d × 10) + (MEDIUM within 30d × 5)
               + (LOW within 28d × 1),
  status       'hard_excluded' / 'manual_review' / 'eligible'

Hard-excluded customers ARE returned (risk-ops audits the queue). Filter to customers whose tier ranks at or above the campaign's min_tier and whose lifecycle_state matches target_state. Sort by risk_score DESC, customer_id ASC.

Schema:
```
customers(customer_id, lifecycle_state, tier, name, email, signup_date, country, city), fraud_signals(customer_id, signal_ts, signal_type, severity), campaigns(campaign_id, name, target_state, min_tier), params(customer_id, lifecycle_state, tier)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS customers;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS fraud_signals;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS campaigns;
CREATE TABLE customers (
    customer_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    lifecycle_state TEXT, tier TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE fraud_signals (
    customer_id INTEGER, signal_ts DATE,
    signal_type TEXT, severity TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE campaigns (
    campaign_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT, target_state TEXT, min_tier TEXT
);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES
  (1,'churned','Tier 1'),(2,'churned','Tier 1'),(3,'churned','Tier 2'),
  (4,'churned','Tier 1'),(5,'churned','Tier 2'),(6,'active','Tier 1'),
  (7,'churned','Tier 3'),(8,'churned','Tier 2');
INSERT INTO fraud_signals VALUES
  (2,'2025-03-02','address_change_burst','HIGH'),
  (3,'2025-04-12','high_return_rate','MEDIUM'),
  (4,'2025-04-08','velocity','LOW'),(4,'2025-04-15','velocity','LOW'),
  (4,'2025-04-22','velocity','LOW'),(4,'2025-04-28','velocity','LOW'),
  (4,'2025-03-26','velocity','MEDIUM'),
  (5,'2025-04-04','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-08','velocity','LOW'),
  (5,'2025-04-12','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-16','velocity','LOW'),
  (5,'2025-04-20','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-24','velocity','LOW'),
  (5,'2025-04-28','velocity','LOW'),(5,'2025-04-06','high_return_rate','MEDIUM'),
  (5,'2025-04-09','address_change_burst','MEDIUM'),
  (8,'2024-09-10','velocity','LOW');
INSERT INTO campaigns VALUES (1,'win_back_q2_2025','churned','Tier 2');

WITH params AS (SELECT DATE '2025-05-01' AS snapshot),
cmp AS (SELECT * FROM campaigns WHERE name = 'win_back_q2_2025'),
sig AS (
    SELECT f.customer_id, f.severity, f.signal_ts,
           ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - f.signal_ts) AS days_back
    FROM fraud_signals f
    WHERE f.signal_ts >= (SELECT snapshot FROM params) - INTERVAL '90 days'
),
sig_days AS (
    SELECT customer_id, signal_ts AS signal_day
    FROM sig
    GROUP BY customer_id, signal_ts
),
sig_indexed AS (
    SELECT customer_id, signal_day,
           (signal_day - DATE '2000-01-01')
             - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY signal_day) AS island_id
    FROM sig_days
),
sig_islands AS (
    SELECT customer_id, island_id, COUNT(*) AS streak_len
    FROM sig_indexed GROUP BY customer_id, island_id
),
burst_per_cust AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MAX(streak_len) AS longest_burst
    FROM sig_islands GROUP BY customer_id
),
fraud_summary AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 7 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l7_signals,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l28_signals,
        SUM(CASE WHEN days_back < 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS l90_signals,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'HIGH' AND days_back <= 90 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS high_l90,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'MEDIUM' AND days_back <= 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS medium_l30,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'LOW' AND days_back <= 28 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS low_l28,
        SUM(CASE WHEN severity='HIGH' AND days_back <= 90 THEN 10
                 WHEN severity='MEDIUM' AND days_back <= 30 THEN 5
                 WHEN severity='LOW' AND days_back <= 28 THEN 1
                 ELSE 0 END) AS risk_score
    FROM sig GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.tier, c.lifecycle_state,
    COALESCE(fs.l7_signals, 0) AS l7_signals,
    COALESCE(fs.l28_signals, 0) AS l28_signals,
    COALESCE(fs.l90_signals, 0) AS l90_signals,
    COALESCE(fs.high_l90, 0) AS high_l90,
    COALESCE(fs.medium_l30, 0) AS medium_l30,
    COALESCE(fs.low_l28, 0) AS low_l28,
    COALESCE(bp.longest_burst, 0) AS longest_burst,
    COALESCE(fs.risk_score, 0) AS risk_score,
    CASE
        WHEN COALESCE(fs.high_l90, 0) > 0
          OR COALESCE(fs.risk_score, 0) >= 15
          OR COALESCE(bp.longest_burst, 0) >= 5 THEN 'hard_excluded'
        WHEN COALESCE(fs.medium_l30, 0) > 0
          OR COALESCE(fs.risk_score, 0) >= 5
          OR COALESCE(bp.longest_burst, 0) >= 3 THEN 'manual_review'
        ELSE 'eligible'
    END AS status
FROM customers c
JOIN cmp ON c.lifecycle_state = cmp.target_state
LEFT JOIN fraud_summary fs ON fs.customer_id = c.customer_id
LEFT JOIN burst_per_cust bp ON bp.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE CAST(substr(c.tier, 6) AS INTEGER) <= CAST(substr(cmp.min_tier, 6) AS INTEGER)
ORDER BY risk_score DESC, c.customer_id ASC;

questions.prompt:
Per-Order Growth Status with Monthly Cohort + MoM Trend

Build a monthly report for the past 6 months where each row is
  year_month, growth_status, customers, prev_month_customers, mom_growth_pct
growth_status ∈ {'NEW','RESURRECTED','RETAINED_REPEAT'}.

  • For each order, label its growth_status using the standard
    rules (no previous order = NEW, gap > 90d = RESURRECTED,
    else RETAINED_REPEAT).
  • Aggregate distinct customers per (year_month, status).
  • Add prev_month_customers via LAG within (status partition).
  • mom_growth_pct = (cur - prev) / prev × 100; NULL if prev is 0.

Sort by year_month DESC, status priority (NEW, RESURRECTED, RETAINED_REPEAT).

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, julianday, labeled, monthly, strftime, trended, year_month, status)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER, order_date DATE, amount NUMERIC
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 1,'2024-08-04', 80),(2, 1,'2024-12-12',100),
  (3, 1,'2025-01-22',110),(4, 1,'2025-03-08',120),
  (5, 2,'2024-11-04',150),(6, 2,'2024-12-22',180),
  (7, 2,'2025-02-14',200),
  (8, 3,'2024-12-09',300),
  (9, 3,'2025-04-12',280),
  (10,4,'2025-01-15',120),(11,4,'2025-02-22',90),(12,4,'2025-03-30',110),
  (13,5,'2025-02-08', 60),
  (14,5,'2025-04-25', 80),
  (15,6,'2024-04-01', 50),(16,6,'2025-03-04', 75),
  (17,7,'2025-03-15',200),
  (18,7,'2025-04-02',220),
  (19,8,'2025-04-08',330);

WITH labeled AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date,
        TO_CHAR(order_date, 'YYYY-MM') AS ym,
        CASE
            WHEN LAG(order_date) OVER w IS NULL THEN 'NEW'
            WHEN (order_date - LAG(order_date) OVER w) > 90 THEN 'RESURRECTED'
            ELSE 'RETAINED_REPEAT'
        END AS growth_status
    FROM orders
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)
),
monthly AS (
    SELECT ym AS year_month, growth_status,
           COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers
    FROM labeled
    WHERE order_date >= DATE '2024-11-01'
    GROUP BY ym, growth_status
),
trended AS (
    SELECT year_month, growth_status, customers,
           LAG(customers) OVER (PARTITION BY growth_status ORDER BY year_month) AS prev_month_customers
    FROM monthly
)
SELECT year_month, growth_status, customers, prev_month_customers,
    CASE WHEN prev_month_customers > 0
         THEN ROUND(100.0*(customers - prev_month_customers)/prev_month_customers, 1)
         END AS mom_growth_pct
FROM trended
ORDER BY year_month DESC,
    CASE growth_status WHEN 'NEW' THEN 1
                       WHEN 'RESURRECTED' THEN 2
                       ELSE 3 END;

questions.prompt:
Consecutive Activity Streaks — Gaps-and-Islands with D1/D7/D28 Retention and Exit Status

Given a user_activity table (user_id, activity_date, activity_type), apply gaps-and-islands to detect every consecutive-day streak per user, then compute:
  user_id, streak_id, streak_start, streak_end, streak_days,
  d1_retained, d7_retained, d28_retained,   -- 1 if next activity within N days
  next_activity_date,                       -- earliest activity after streak_end
  exit_status ∈ {'continuing','resurrected','long_resurrect',
                 'churned','open'}

  • 'continuing'      — d28_retained = 1
  • 'resurrected'     — next activity is 28-90d after streak_end
  • 'long_resurrect'  — next activity is > 90d after streak_end
  • 'churned'         — no further activity AND streak ended > 90d ago
  • 'open'            — no further activity yet, still in observation window

Snapshot date is 2025-04-01. Sort by user_id ASC, streak_id ASC.

Schema:
```
user_activity(activity_id, user_id, activity_date, activity_type), params(activity_id, user_id, activity_date, activity_type), next_activity(streak_id, user_id)
```
questions.model_answer:
-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Setup — DATE-typed activity_date so the streak / island / D1 │
-- │  / D7 / D28 retention math runs natively in PostgreSQL.       │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_activity;
CREATE TABLE user_activity (
    activity_id   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id       INTEGER,
    activity_date DATE,
    activity_type TEXT
);
INSERT INTO user_activity VALUES
  -- Customer 1: two streaks (Mar 1-5, Mar 12-14) → D7-retained
  (1,1,'2025-03-01','login'),(2,1,'2025-03-02','login'),(3,1,'2025-03-03','order'),
  (4,1,'2025-03-04','login'),(5,1,'2025-03-05','login'),
  (6,1,'2025-03-12','login'),(7,1,'2025-03-13','login'),(8,1,'2025-03-14','order'),
  -- Customer 2: long_resurrect (Dec 10-12 → Mar 26-27 = 105-day gap)
  (9, 2,'2024-12-10','login'),(10,2,'2024-12-11','login'),(11,2,'2024-12-12','order'),
  (12,2,'2025-03-26','login'),(13,2,'2025-03-27','order'),
  -- Customer 3: single streak in Nov, snapshot is Apr → churned
  (14,3,'2024-11-20','login'),(15,3,'2024-11-21','login'),(16,3,'2024-11-22','order'),
  (17,3,'2024-11-23','login'),
  -- Customer 4: open streak (Mar 28-29, no follow-up before snapshot)
  (18,4,'2025-03-28','login'),(19,4,'2025-03-29','login'),
  -- Customer 5: alternating-day pattern (no streak ≥ 2 consecutive days)
  (20,5,'2025-03-10','login'),(21,5,'2025-03-12','login'),
  (22,5,'2025-03-14','login'),(23,5,'2025-03-16','login'),
  -- Customer 6: resurrected (Feb 1-3, then Mar 25 = 50-day gap)
  (24,6,'2025-02-01','login'),(25,6,'2025-02-02','login'),(26,6,'2025-02-03','order'),
  (27,6,'2025-03-25','login');

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Solution — gaps-and-islands streaks per user, then D1/D7/D28│
-- │  retention + 5-bucket exit_status (continuing / resurrected /│
-- │  long_resurrect / churned / open).                            │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH params(snapshot) AS (VALUES (DATE '2025-04-01')),
days AS (
    SELECT user_id, activity_date
    FROM user_activity
    WHERE activity_date >= (SELECT snapshot FROM params) - INTERVAL '540 days'
    GROUP BY user_id, activity_date
),
indexed AS (
    -- Island id = date - row_number(): consecutive dates collapse to the
    -- same island_id, gaps break it. Cast ROW_NUMBER to INT so PG accepts
    -- `date - integer`.
    SELECT
        user_id, activity_date,
        activity_date - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY activity_date))::INT
            AS island_id
    FROM days
),
streaks AS (
    SELECT
        user_id, island_id,
        MIN(activity_date) AS streak_start,
        MAX(activity_date) AS streak_end,
        COUNT(*)           AS streak_days,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY MIN(activity_date))
            AS streak_id
    FROM indexed
    GROUP BY user_id, island_id
),
next_activity AS (
    SELECT
        s.user_id, s.streak_id, s.streak_end,
        MIN(d.activity_date) AS next_activity_date
    FROM streaks s
    LEFT JOIN days d
      ON d.user_id = s.user_id
     AND d.activity_date > s.streak_end
    GROUP BY s.user_id, s.streak_id, s.streak_end
),
enriched AS (
    SELECT
        s.user_id, s.streak_id, s.streak_start, s.streak_end, s.streak_days,
        ((SELECT snapshot FROM params) - s.streak_end) AS days_since_streak_end,
        n.next_activity_date,
        CASE WHEN n.next_activity_date IS NOT NULL
              AND (n.next_activity_date - s.streak_end) <= 1   THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS d1_retained,
        CASE WHEN n.next_activity_date IS NOT NULL
              AND (n.next_activity_date - s.streak_end) <= 7   THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS d7_retained,
        CASE WHEN n.next_activity_date IS NOT NULL
              AND (n.next_activity_date - s.streak_end) <= 28  THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS d28_retained
    FROM streaks s
    LEFT JOIN next_activity n
      ON n.user_id = s.user_id AND n.streak_id = s.streak_id
)
SELECT
    user_id, streak_id, streak_start, streak_end, streak_days,
    d1_retained, d7_retained, d28_retained, next_activity_date,
    CASE
        WHEN d28_retained = 1                                          THEN 'continuing'
        WHEN next_activity_date IS NULL AND days_since_streak_end > 90 THEN 'churned'
        WHEN next_activity_date IS NULL                                THEN 'open'
        WHEN (next_activity_date - streak_end) > 90                    THEN 'long_resurrect'
        ELSE                                                                'resurrected'
    END AS exit_status
FROM enriched
ORDER BY user_id, streak_id;

questions.prompt:
Tier Stability Streaks — DIFFERENCE-Method Gaps-and-Islands on Monthly RFM

Given a monthly_tier table (already computed elsewhere), use the DIFFERENCE method (two ROW_NUMBERs) to detect every consecutive same-tier streak per customer:

  streak_id = ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY ym)
             - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id, tier ORDER BY ym)

The trick: both row numbers march upward in lock-step inside a same-tier run, so their difference is constant within each streak; any tier change shifts the difference.

Return per customer:
  customer_id, current_tier, current_streak_months,
  longest_tier, longest_streak_months,
  longest_streak_start, longest_streak_end,
  total_distinct_streaks   — how many tier transitions in their history

Sort by longest_streak_months DESC, customer_id ASC.

Schema:
```
monthly_tier(customer_id, ym, tier), longest(customer_id, longest_streak_end, longest_streak_months, longest_streak_start, longest_tier), current_streak(current_streak_months, current_tier, customer_id)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS monthly_tier;
CREATE TABLE monthly_tier (
    customer_id INTEGER,
    ym          TEXT,    -- YYYY-MM
    tier        TEXT,    -- 'Tier 1' / 'Tier 2' / 'Tier 3'
    PRIMARY KEY (customer_id, ym)
);
INSERT INTO monthly_tier VALUES
  -- C1: long Tier-1 run, recently dropped → urgent at-risk
  (1,'2024-01','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-02','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-03','Tier 1'),
  (1,'2024-04','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-05','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-06','Tier 1'),
  (1,'2024-07','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-08','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-09','Tier 1'),
  (1,'2024-10','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-11','Tier 1'),(1,'2024-12','Tier 1'),
  (1,'2025-01','Tier 2'),(1,'2025-02','Tier 2'),(1,'2025-03','Tier 2'),
  -- C2: oscillator — many short streaks, longest only 2 months
  (2,'2024-01','Tier 2'),(2,'2024-02','Tier 1'),(2,'2024-03','Tier 1'),
  (2,'2024-04','Tier 2'),(2,'2024-05','Tier 1'),(2,'2024-06','Tier 2'),
  (2,'2024-07','Tier 2'),(2,'2024-08','Tier 1'),(2,'2024-09','Tier 1'),
  (2,'2024-10','Tier 2'),(2,'2024-11','Tier 3'),(2,'2024-12','Tier 2'),
  (2,'2025-01','Tier 2'),(2,'2025-02','Tier 1'),(2,'2025-03','Tier 1'),
  -- C3: steady Tier-2 climb to Tier-1 (two streaks)
  (3,'2024-06','Tier 3'),(3,'2024-07','Tier 3'),(3,'2024-08','Tier 3'),
  (3,'2024-09','Tier 2'),(3,'2024-10','Tier 2'),(3,'2024-11','Tier 2'),
  (3,'2024-12','Tier 2'),(3,'2025-01','Tier 1'),(3,'2025-02','Tier 1'),
  (3,'2025-03','Tier 1'),
  -- C4: brand-new repeat customer (3 months only)
  (4,'2025-01','Tier 3'),(4,'2025-02','Tier 2'),(4,'2025-03','Tier 2');

-- ── DIFFERENCE METHOD ─────────────────────────────────────────────
-- For a streak of consecutive months in the same tier:
--   rn_all  = ROW_NUMBER() over the customer's full timeline (1, 2, 3 …)
--   rn_tier = ROW_NUMBER() over (customer, tier) timeline       (1, 2, 3 …)
-- Inside a same-tier run BOTH increase by 1 in lock-step, so their
-- difference is constant. Any tier change resets the spread, opening a
-- fresh streak_id. This is the canonical "two ROW_NUMBERs" trick.
WITH diffed AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        ym,
        tier,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id          ORDER BY ym) -
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id, tier    ORDER BY ym) AS streak_id
    FROM monthly_tier
),
streaks AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        tier,
        streak_id,
        COUNT(*)          AS streak_months,
        MIN(ym)           AS streak_start,
        MAX(ym)           AS streak_end
    FROM diffed
    GROUP BY customer_id, tier, streak_id
),
-- Longest streak per customer (one row per customer)
longest AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        tier            AS longest_tier,
        streak_months   AS longest_streak_months,
        streak_start    AS longest_streak_start,
        streak_end      AS longest_streak_end,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id
                           ORDER BY streak_months DESC, streak_end DESC) AS rn
    FROM streaks
),
-- Current streak — the streak that contains each customer's MAX(ym)
current_streak AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        tier            AS current_tier,
        streak_months   AS current_streak_months,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY streak_end DESC) AS rn
    FROM streaks
),
streak_count AS (
    SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS total_distinct_streaks
    FROM streaks
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT
    l.customer_id,
    c.current_tier,
    c.current_streak_months,
    l.longest_tier,
    l.longest_streak_months,
    l.longest_streak_start,
    l.longest_streak_end,
    sc.total_distinct_streaks
FROM longest l
JOIN current_streak c ON c.customer_id = l.customer_id AND c.rn = 1
JOIN streak_count   sc ON sc.customer_id = l.customer_id
WHERE l.rn = 1
ORDER BY l.longest_streak_months DESC, l.customer_id ASC;

questions.prompt:
Sessionize Raw Clickstream — Cumulative-Flag Sessionization with 30-Min Gap Rule

Given the raw events table, sessionize per user with a 30-minute inactivity timeout. For every reconstructed session, return:

  user_id, session_id, session_start, session_end,
  duration_seconds, event_count, distinct_event_types,
  has_purchase   — 1 if any event_type = 'purchase' in the session,
  bounce         — 1 if event_count = 1

Sort by user_id ASC, session_id ASC.

Schema:
```
events(event_id, user_id, event_ts, event_type)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS events;
CREATE TABLE events (
    event_id   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id    INTEGER,
    event_ts   TEXT,    -- 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'
    event_type TEXT
);
INSERT INTO events VALUES
  -- U1: two sessions on Mar 28; the second includes a purchase
  (1,1,'2025-03-28 09:00:05','view'),
  (2,1,'2025-03-28 09:02:30','detail_view'),
  (3,1,'2025-03-28 09:08:15','add_to_cart'),
  (4,1,'2025-03-28 09:25:00','view'),
  -- 45-min gap → new session
  (5,1,'2025-03-28 10:10:00','view'),
  (6,1,'2025-03-28 10:18:30','add_to_cart'),
  (7,1,'2025-03-28 10:24:00','checkout'),
  (8,1,'2025-03-28 10:31:15','purchase'),
  -- U2: one bounce + one normal session
  (9,2,'2025-03-28 12:00:00','view'),
  -- 90-min gap → new session
  (10,2,'2025-03-28 13:30:00','view'),
  (11,2,'2025-03-28 13:45:00','detail_view'),
  (12,2,'2025-03-28 14:00:30','review_read'),
  (13,2,'2025-03-28 14:14:45','add_to_cart'),
  -- U3: long evening session (gaps under 30 min throughout)
  (14,3,'2025-03-28 19:00:00','view'),
  (15,3,'2025-03-28 19:15:00','view'),
  (16,3,'2025-03-28 19:42:00','detail_view'),
  (17,3,'2025-03-28 20:05:00','wishlist_add'),
  (18,3,'2025-03-28 20:32:00','view'),
  (19,3,'2025-03-28 21:00:00','add_to_cart');

-- ── CUMULATIVE-FLAG SESSIONIZATION ──────────────────────────────
-- Step 1: mark every event whose gap from the previous event is > 30
-- minutes (or which has no previous event) as a session boundary.
-- Step 2: a running SUM of that flag, partitioned by user, gives every
-- event a deterministic session_id — no self-join, single pass.
WITH flagged AS (
    SELECT
        e.event_id,
        e.user_id,
        e.event_ts,
        e.event_type,
        CASE
          WHEN LAG(e.event_ts) OVER w IS NULL                                   THEN 1
          WHEN (julianday(e.event_ts) - julianday(LAG(e.event_ts) OVER w))
                  * 24 * 60 > 30                                                THEN 1
          ELSE 0
        END AS new_session
    FROM events e
    WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY e.user_id ORDER BY e.event_ts)
),
sessionized AS (
    SELECT
        user_id,
        event_ts,
        event_type,
        SUM(new_session) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_ts
                               ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
            AS session_id
    FROM flagged
)
SELECT
    user_id,
    session_id,
    MIN(event_ts) AS session_start,
    MAX(event_ts) AS session_end,
    CAST((julianday(MAX(event_ts)) - julianday(MIN(event_ts))) * 86400 AS INTEGER)
        AS duration_seconds,
    COUNT(*)                                          AS event_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT event_type)                        AS distinct_event_types,
    MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'purchase' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_purchase,
    CASE WHEN COUNT(*) = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END          AS bounce
FROM sessionized
GROUP BY user_id, session_id
ORDER BY user_id, session_id;

questions.prompt:
Consecutive-Day Activity Streaks With In-Stream Zero-Code Days

Given user_activity_log restricted to dates between 2014-09-01 and 2014-09-11, group rows into consecutive-day islands per user (any row, even user_code = 0, counts as 'in-stream'). For each island return:
  user_name, start_date, end_date, result
where:
  • start_date = first calendar day in the island whose user_code <> 0
  • end_date   = last calendar day in the island whose user_code <> 0
  • result     = COUNT of days with user_code <> 0

Show only islands with result ≥ 2. Sort by user_name ASC, start_date ASC.

Expected on the canonical sample:
  user_name | start_date | end_date   | result
  user1     | 2014-09-01 | 2014-09-03 | 2
  user1     | 2014-09-08 | 2014-09-11 | 3
  user2     | 2014-09-04 | 2014-09-07 | 3

Schema:
```
user_activity_log(user_name, user_date, user_code)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_activity_log;
CREATE TABLE user_activity_log (
    user_name TEXT,
    user_date TEXT,
    user_code INTEGER
);
-- Canonical test data (mirrors the interview prompt exactly).
INSERT INTO user_activity_log VALUES
  ('user1','2014-08-31',232),
  ('user1','2014-09-01',232),
  ('user1','2014-09-02',  0),
  ('user1','2014-09-03',121),
  ('user1','2014-09-08',122),
  ('user1','2014-09-09',  0),
  ('user1','2014-09-10',144),
  ('user1','2014-09-11',166),
  ('user2','2014-09-01',177),
  ('user2','2014-09-04',188),
  ('user2','2014-09-05',199),
  ('user2','2014-09-06',  0),
  ('user2','2014-09-07',155);

-- ── Gaps-and-islands on the activity-day grid ──────────────────
-- Step 1: filter to the report window. The 08-31 row falls outside
-- the [09-01, 09-11] range, so it doesn't contaminate the per-user
-- ROW_NUMBER sequence used by the islands trick below.
-- Step 2: classic value − ROW_NUMBER() islands. julianday(user_date)
-- and ROW_NUMBER() both increase by exactly 1 in lock-step inside a
-- contiguous-day run, so their difference is constant per island.
-- Note that user_code = 0 rows ARE present in the stream — they
-- silently slip into the surrounding island and don't break the
-- ROW_NUMBER sequence. Only a calendar gap (a missing day) opens
-- a new island.
-- Step 3: per island, COUNT non-zero rows (the result) and the
-- MIN / MAX dates among non-zero rows (the start / end the
-- marketing team actually cares about).
WITH filtered AS (
    SELECT
        user_name,
        user_date,
        user_code,
        CAST(julianday(user_date) AS INTEGER) AS jd
    FROM user_activity_log
    WHERE user_date BETWEEN '2014-09-01' AND '2014-09-11'
),
indexed AS (
    SELECT
        user_name, user_date, user_code, jd,
        jd - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_name ORDER BY user_date)
            AS island
    FROM filtered
),
islands AS (
    SELECT
        user_name,
        island,
        MIN(CASE WHEN user_code <> 0 THEN user_date END)         AS start_date,
        MAX(CASE WHEN user_code <> 0 THEN user_date END)         AS end_date,
        SUM(CASE WHEN user_code <> 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)          AS result
    FROM indexed
    GROUP BY user_name, island
)
SELECT user_name, start_date, end_date, result
FROM islands
WHERE result >= 2
ORDER BY user_name ASC, start_date ASC;

questions.prompt:
Daily Snapshot-Merge — Today vs Yesterday Liveness State Machine

Snapshot date is 2026-05-05 ('today'). Build the daily growth-accounting report by joining today_l1 with the user_state_master (yesterday's state) and emit two result sets that the morning batch writes to the warehouse:

1. Per-user growth_status row:
   user_id | growth_status | new_streak | tier_carry
   growth_status ∈ {'NEW','RESURRECTED','RETAINED','CHURNED','STALE'}
     • NEW          — no row in user_state_master
     • RESURRECTED  — active today, was inactive yesterday
     • RETAINED     — active today AND yesterday
     • CHURNED      — inactive today, was active yesterday
     • STALE        — inactive today AND yesterday (still in master)
   new_streak     — current_streak + 1 if active today, else 0
   tier_carry     — tier from yesterday (so the report can
                    diff against tomorrow's recomputed tier)

2. Net momentum summary row:
   growth_status | customers
   for ('NEW','RESURRECTED','RETAINED','CHURNED'). Use this to compute Net Growth = (new + resurrected) − churned.

Assume today_l1 is built from a single-day partition prune of watch_history (tiny scan). Assume user_state_master is the previous morning's output. Sort the per-user rows by growth_status (NEW, RESURRECTED, RETAINED, CHURNED, STALE) then by user_id; sort the summary by the same order.

Schema:
```
watch_history(user_id, watch_date), user_state_master(user_id, last_active_date, is_active_yesterday, current_streak, tier), merged(user_id, watch_date)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS watch_history;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_state_master;

CREATE TABLE watch_history (
    user_id    INTEGER,
    watch_date TEXT
);
-- A few days of history; the pipeline only ever scans a SINGLE day
-- partition (today) — so even billions of historical rows are free.
INSERT INTO watch_history VALUES
  (1, '2026-05-04'), (2, '2026-05-04'), (3, '2026-05-04'), (4, '2026-05-04'),
  -- Today's activity ('2026-05-05'):
  (1, '2026-05-05'),  -- U1: active today, was active yesterday → RETAINED
  (2, '2026-05-05'),  -- U2: active today, was active yesterday → RETAINED
  (5, '2026-05-05'),  -- U5: active today, NOT in master → NEW
  (6, '2026-05-05');  -- U6: active today, master says inactive → RESURRECTED

CREATE TABLE user_state_master (
    user_id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    last_active_date     TEXT,
    is_active_yesterday  INTEGER,   -- 1 if active in the prior batch
    current_streak       INTEGER,
    tier                 TEXT
);
INSERT INTO user_state_master VALUES
  -- yesterday's morning batch wrote these rows:
  (1, '2026-05-04', 1, 7,  'Tier 1'),  -- active yesterday → RETAINED today
  (2, '2026-05-04', 1, 3,  'Tier 2'),  -- active yesterday → RETAINED today
  (3, '2026-05-04', 1, 12, 'Tier 1'),  -- active yesterday, inactive today → CHURNED
  (4, '2026-05-04', 1, 1,  'Tier 3'),  -- active yesterday, inactive today → CHURNED
  (6, '2026-04-22', 0, 0,  'Tier 2'),  -- inactive yesterday, active today → RESURRECTED
  (7, '2026-03-01', 0, 0,  'Tier 3');  -- inactive both days → STALE

-- ── PERF rewrite ─────────────────────────────────────────
--   Step A: today_l1 — single-day partition prune of watch_history.
--   This is the WHOLE reason the pattern scales: we never re-scan
--   history; we only scan TODAY. GROUP BY (not DISTINCT) so the
--   planner pushes the dedupe into the scan.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WITH today_l1 AS (
    SELECT user_id, MAX(watch_date) AS last_login
    FROM watch_history
    WHERE watch_date = '2026-05-05'    -- partition prune
    GROUP BY user_id
),
-- Step B: FULL OUTER JOIN today's L1 against yesterday's state.
-- The JOIN key is user_id on both sides — both inputs are unique
-- per user_id, so the planner picks a hash join on the small
-- today_l1 set. NO global sort, NO DISTINCT, NO history rescan.
merged AS (
    SELECT
        COALESCE(t.user_id, s.user_id) AS user_id,
        t.user_id IS NOT NULL          AS active_today,
        s.user_id IS NOT NULL          AS in_master,
        s.is_active_yesterday          AS active_yesterday,
        s.current_streak               AS prev_streak,
        s.tier                         AS prev_tier
    FROM user_state_master s
    FULL OUTER JOIN today_l1 t ON s.user_id = t.user_id
),
labelled AS (
    SELECT
        user_id,
        CASE
            WHEN NOT in_master AND active_today                              THEN 'NEW'
            WHEN active_today AND COALESCE(active_yesterday, 0) = 0          THEN 'RESURRECTED'
            WHEN active_today AND COALESCE(active_yesterday, 0) = 1          THEN 'RETAINED'
            WHEN NOT active_today AND COALESCE(active_yesterday, 0) = 1      THEN 'CHURNED'
            ELSE                                                                  'STALE'
        END AS growth_status,
        CASE
            WHEN active_today THEN COALESCE(prev_streak, 0) + 1
            ELSE 0
        END AS new_streak,
        prev_tier AS tier_carry
    FROM merged
)
-- Per-user output. The same query writes BOTH the daily report and
-- (after a small projection) tomorrow's user_state_master, so the
-- whole pipeline is one shuffle.
SELECT
    user_id,
    growth_status,
    new_streak,
    tier_carry
FROM labelled
ORDER BY
    CASE growth_status
        WHEN 'NEW'         THEN 1
        WHEN 'RESURRECTED' THEN 2
        WHEN 'RETAINED'    THEN 3
        WHEN 'CHURNED'     THEN 4
        ELSE                    5
    END,
    user_id;

-- ── Companion query: net-momentum summary ─────────────────────────
-- Same merged CTE, just rolled up. In the production pipeline both
-- result sets fall out of the same DAG node — the summary is
-- essentially free once `labelled` is materialised.
-- WITH ... (same labelled CTE as above)
-- SELECT growth_status, COUNT(*) AS customers
-- FROM labelled
-- WHERE growth_status IN ('NEW','RESURRECTED','RETAINED','CHURNED')
-- GROUP BY growth_status
-- ORDER BY CASE growth_status
--     WHEN 'NEW' THEN 1 WHEN 'RESURRECTED' THEN 2
--     WHEN 'RETAINED' THEN 3 ELSE 4 END;

-- Net Growth = (NEW + RESURRECTED) − CHURNED.
-- If today_l1 is consistently smaller than the CHURNED segment,
-- the store is shrinking regardless of how aggressive marketing is.

questions.prompt:
CFO Growth Accounting — Revenue Flow With L1/L7/L28 Velocity and ARPU per Bucket

Today is 2026-05-06. Build the CFO Growth-Accounting Report:

  growth_status | user_count | total_revenue | total_qty |
  arpu          | l1_users   | l7_users      | l28_users

Definitions:
  • growth_status ∈ {'NEW','RETAINED','RESURRECTED','CHURNED'}
    NEW         — first time seen (no row in user_state_master)
    RETAINED    — active today AND was_active_l28 = 1
    RESURRECTED — active today AND was_active_l28 = 0
    CHURNED     — NOT active today AND was_active_l28 = 1
    STALE rows (inactive both) are excluded from the report.
  • arpu = total_revenue / user_count  (NULL for CHURNED)
  • L-window counts are derived from `today - last_active_date`:
      l1_users  — gap = 0 (active today)
      l7_users  — gap ≤ 7
      l28_users — gap ≤ 28
    NEW customers count toward l1 by definition.

Sort by growth_status (NEW, RETAINED, RESURRECTED, CHURNED).

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, user_id, order_date, amount, customer_id, status), user_state_master(user_id, last_active_date, is_active_l28, total_ltv)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_state_master;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INTEGER, order_date DATE, amount NUMERIC
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 501, '2026-05-05', 200), (2, 502, '2026-04-01', 90),
  (3, 501, '2026-05-06', 150),(4, 502, '2026-05-06', 80),
  (5, 503, '2026-05-06', 60),(6, 504, '2026-05-06', 90),
  (7, 505, '2026-05-06', 220),(8, 506, '2026-05-06', 30);
CREATE TABLE user_state_master (
    user_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    last_active_date DATE, is_active_l28 INTEGER, total_ltv NUMERIC
);
INSERT INTO user_state_master VALUES
  (501, '2026-05-05', 1, 1450.00),
  (502, '2026-04-01', 0,  120.00),
  (503, '2026-04-30', 1,  600.00),
  (504, '2026-04-12', 1,  330.00),
  (507, '2026-05-04', 1,  900.00),
  (508, '2026-05-03', 1,  250.00),
  (509, '2026-01-15', 0, 1500.00);

WITH today_activity AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) AS today_spend, COUNT(order_id) AS today_qty
    FROM orders WHERE order_date = DATE '2026-05-06'
    GROUP BY user_id
),
growth_merge AS (
    SELECT
        COALESCE(t.user_id, s.user_id) AS user_id,
        t.today_spend, t.today_qty,
        s.last_active_date, s.is_active_l28,
        CASE
            WHEN s.user_id IS NULL THEN 'NEW'
            WHEN t.user_id IS NOT NULL AND COALESCE(s.is_active_l28,0) = 1 THEN 'RETAINED'
            WHEN t.user_id IS NOT NULL AND COALESCE(s.is_active_l28,0) = 0 THEN 'RESURRECTED'
            WHEN t.user_id IS NULL AND COALESCE(s.is_active_l28,0) = 1 THEN 'CHURNED'
            ELSE 'STALE'
        END AS growth_status,
        CASE WHEN t.user_id IS NOT NULL THEN 0
             ELSE (DATE '2026-05-06' - s.last_active_date)
        END AS days_since_active
    FROM user_state_master s
    FULL OUTER JOIN today_activity t ON s.user_id = t.user_id
)
SELECT growth_status,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS user_count,
    COALESCE(ROUND(SUM(today_spend), 2), 0) AS total_revenue,
    COALESCE(SUM(today_qty), 0) AS total_qty,
    CASE WHEN COALESCE(SUM(today_spend), 0) = 0 THEN NULL
         ELSE ROUND(SUM(today_spend) / COUNT(DISTINCT user_id), 2) END AS arpu,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FILTER (WHERE days_since_active = 0) AS l1_users,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FILTER (WHERE days_since_active <= 7) AS l7_users,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FILTER (WHERE days_since_active <= 28) AS l28_users
FROM growth_merge
WHERE growth_status <> 'STALE'
GROUP BY growth_status
ORDER BY CASE growth_status
    WHEN 'NEW' THEN 1 WHEN 'RETAINED' THEN 2
    WHEN 'RESURRECTED' THEN 3 WHEN 'CHURNED' THEN 4
END;

questions.prompt:
NTILE Decile Velocity Cohorts — Exact Top-10 % vs PERCENT_RANK Continuous Rank

Snapshot 2025-04-01. Per customer with at least 2 paid orders in the last 90 days, compute:
  customer_id, l90_orders, l90_spend,
  velocity_decile     — NTILE(10) over l90_spend DESC (1 = top 10 %)
  pct_rank            — PERCENT_RANK over l90_spend DESC
  cohort              — 'top_10' if decile = 1,
                        'next_15' if decile in (2,3) and pct_rank ≤ 0.25,
                        'mid_50'  if decile in (3..7),
                        'bottom_25' otherwise.

Sort by velocity_decile ASC, l90_spend DESC.

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), params(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER,
    order_date  TEXT,
    amount      REAL
);
-- 20 customers spanning a wide spend distribution so NTILE produces
-- visibly different bucket sizes from PERCENT_RANK.
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 1,'2025-03-28',2000),(2, 1,'2025-03-15',1800),(3, 1,'2025-02-28',1900),
  (4, 2,'2025-03-25',1500),(5, 2,'2025-03-10',1600),
  (6, 3,'2025-03-22',1300),(7, 3,'2025-03-01',1200),
  (8, 4,'2025-03-20',1100),(9, 4,'2025-02-25',1000),
  (10,5,'2025-03-18', 900),(11,5,'2025-02-20', 950),
  (12,6,'2025-03-15', 800),(13,6,'2025-02-15', 850),
  (14,7,'2025-03-12', 700),(15,7,'2025-02-10', 750),
  (16,8,'2025-03-09', 600),(17,8,'2025-02-05', 650),
  (18,9,'2025-03-06', 500),(19,9,'2025-01-28', 550),
  (20,10,'2025-03-03', 400),(21,10,'2025-01-25', 450),
  (22,11,'2025-03-01', 350),(23,11,'2025-01-22', 380),
  (24,12,'2025-02-26', 300),(25,12,'2025-01-19', 320),
  (26,13,'2025-02-23', 250),(27,13,'2025-01-16', 270),
  (28,14,'2025-02-20', 200),(29,14,'2025-01-13', 220),
  (30,15,'2025-02-17', 170),(31,15,'2025-01-10', 180),
  (32,16,'2025-02-14', 140),(33,16,'2025-01-07', 150),
  (34,17,'2025-02-11', 110),(35,17,'2025-01-04', 120),
  (36,18,'2025-02-08',  90),(37,18,'2025-01-01', 100),
  (38,19,'2025-02-05',  60),(39,19,'2024-12-29',  70),
  (40,20,'2025-02-02',  30),(41,20,'2024-12-26',  40);

-- ── PERF rationale ───────────────────────────────────────
--   NTILE(10) is the right pick here, NOT PERCENT_RANK:
--     • NTILE guarantees equally-sized buckets — the 'top 10 %'
--       cohort always has ceil(n/10) customers, which is what the
--       support-prioritisation budget assumes.
--     • PERCENT_RANK is continuous and is great for relative ranking
--       (RFM Q1 uses it to compute composite tiers), but on a long-
--       tailed distribution it can put 30 % of customers above 0.90
--       — useless for cohort budgets.
--     • A senior tip: ALWAYS show both deciles AND a continuous
--       percentile so the consumer can pick the framing that matches
--       the decision (decile for fixed budget, pct for trend).
--   Date-range WHERE prunes the partition; we never scan history.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WITH params(snapshot) AS (VALUES ('2025-04-01')),
agg AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*)              AS l90_orders,
        ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS l90_spend
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= date((SELECT snapshot FROM params), '-90 days')
      AND order_date <  (SELECT snapshot FROM params)
    GROUP BY customer_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id, l90_orders, l90_spend,
        NTILE(10)      OVER (ORDER BY l90_spend DESC) AS velocity_decile,
        ROUND(PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY l90_spend ASC), 3)
            AS pct_rank
    FROM agg
)
SELECT
    customer_id, l90_orders, l90_spend,
    velocity_decile,
    pct_rank,
    CASE
        WHEN velocity_decile = 1                                 THEN 'top_10'
        WHEN velocity_decile IN (2, 3) AND pct_rank >= 0.75      THEN 'next_15'
        WHEN velocity_decile BETWEEN 3 AND 7                     THEN 'mid_50'
        ELSE                                                          'bottom_25'
    END AS cohort
FROM ranked
ORDER BY velocity_decile ASC, l90_spend DESC;

questions.prompt:
Salting a Skewed Aggregation — The Classic Pareto-Distribution Rescue

Compute total_spend per customer, but write the query in the salted two-stage form so the shuffle is balanced even when one customer owns most of the rows.

  Stage 1: GROUP BY (customer_id, salt) where salt is a small
           uniform integer in [0, N). N = 16 is a sensible
           default (one salt bucket per CPU on a typical worker).
  Stage 2: GROUP BY customer_id over the partials.

Output: customer_id, salt_buckets_seen, total_spend.
Sort by total_spend DESC.

Schema:
```
orders(order_id, customer_id, amount, order_date, status)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER,
    amount      NUMERIC
);
-- Pareto: customer 1 has 30 rows, the long tail has 1-3 rows each.
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1,1,100),(2,1,110),(3,1,120),(4,1,130),(5,1,140),
  (6,1,150),(7,1,160),(8,1,170),(9,1,180),(10,1,190),
  (11,1,200),(12,1,210),(13,1,220),(14,1,230),(15,1,240),
  (16,1,250),(17,1,260),(18,1,270),(19,1,280),(20,1,290),
  (21,1,300),(22,1,310),(23,1,320),(24,1,330),(25,1,340),
  (26,1,350),(27,1,360),(28,1,370),(29,1,380),(30,1,390),
  (31,2,500),(32,2,520),
  (33,3,400),
  (34,4,300),(35,4,310),(36,4,330),
  (37,5,200),
  (38,6,100),(39,6,110);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Salt = uniform int in [0, 16). FLOOR(RANDOM() * 16)::INT │
-- │  is the portable Postgres form. The two-stage aggregation │
-- │  spreads the hot key across 16 reducers.                  │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH salted AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
           FLOOR(RANDOM() * 16)::INT AS salt,
           amount
    FROM orders
),
partial_agg AS (
    SELECT customer_id, salt,
           SUM(amount) AS partial_sum,
           COUNT(*)    AS partial_rows
    FROM salted
    GROUP BY customer_id, salt
)
SELECT customer_id,
       COUNT(*)                   AS salt_buckets_seen,
       ROUND(SUM(partial_sum), 2) AS total_spend
FROM partial_agg
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY total_spend DESC;

questions.prompt:
DAU / MAU With APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT — HyperLogLog at Scale

Build a daily report for the past 7 days with one row per day:
  event_date | dau_exact | dau_approx | absolute_error
Show the same query in two flavours — the exact COUNT(DISTINCT) for small / medium tables, and the APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT (or BigQuery's APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT, Spark's approx_count_distinct, Snowflake's HLL) variant for billion-row tables. SQLite has no native HLL; use the exact count as the runnable result and document the production form inline.

Schema:
```
events(user_id, event_ts)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS events;
CREATE TABLE events (
    user_id  INTEGER,
    event_ts DATE
);
-- Postgres-native: explode 7 days × 700 events using generate_series.
INSERT INTO events
SELECT (FLOOR(RANDOM() * 5000) + 1)::INT, d::DATE
FROM generate_series(DATE '2025-03-25', DATE '2025-03-31', INTERVAL '1 day') AS d
CROSS JOIN generate_series(1, 100);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  PERF rationale —                                          │
-- │    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) builds a hash set of every     │
-- │    unique id in memory. For billions of rows this is the   │
-- │    single most expensive op. APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT (HLL)   │
-- │    estimates with O(log log n) memory and ~2% error.       │
-- │  PGlite doesn't ship HLL out of the box, so this query    │
-- │  shows the EXACT count alongside what you'd swap for HLL  │
-- │  in production (Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift/Trino).       │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
SELECT
    event_ts AS event_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS dau_exact,
    -- Production swap-in:
    --   APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(user_id)  AS dau_approx
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS dau_approx,
    0                       AS absolute_error
FROM events
GROUP BY event_ts
ORDER BY event_ts;

questions.prompt:
Top-3 Products per Customer — QUALIFY Filter on a Window Result

Per customer, return their top 3 products by total spend (qty × unit_price summed across orders):
  customer_id, product_id, total_spend, rk
where rk is the dense rank within the customer's product list. Show the SQLite-runnable form (CTE + WHERE rk ≤ 3) and document the QUALIFY equivalent inline. Sort by customer_id ASC, rk ASC.

Schema:
```
order_items(order_id, customer_id, product_id, qty, unit_price, per_product, ranked)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS order_items;
CREATE TABLE order_items (
    order_id    INTEGER,
    customer_id INTEGER,
    product_id  INTEGER,
    qty         INTEGER,
    unit_price  NUMERIC
);
INSERT INTO order_items VALUES
  -- C1: 5 different products
  (1,1,101,2,30.0),(2,1,101,1,30.0),
  (3,1,102,1,80.0),
  (4,1,103,3,15.0),
  (5,1,104,5,5.0),
  (6,1,105,1,40.0),
  -- C2: 4 products
  (7,2,201,2,50.0),(8,2,201,1,50.0),
  (9,2,202,1,100.0),
  (10,2,203,4,10.0),
  (11,2,204,2,25.0);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Top-3 products per customer using DENSE_RANK on the      │
-- │  total spend per (customer, product). ROUND on NUMERIC    │
-- │  works in Postgres natively.                              │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH per_product AS (
    SELECT customer_id, product_id,
           ROUND(SUM(qty * unit_price), 2) AS total_spend
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY customer_id, product_id
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT customer_id, product_id, total_spend,
           DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id
                              ORDER BY total_spend DESC) AS rk
    FROM per_product
)
SELECT customer_id, product_id, total_spend, rk
FROM ranked
WHERE rk <= 3
ORDER BY customer_id ASC, rk ASC;

questions.prompt:
Watched X But Never Y — Anti-Join with the NOT IN NULL Trap

Using the viewing_activity table, find every user_id that watched 'Stranger Things' at least once but has never watched 'The Witcher'. Then show three solution shapes side-by-side so you can articulate WHY one of them silently breaks:
  (1) NOT IN — the trap when the subquery contains any NULL
  (2) NOT EXISTS — the null-safe form
  (3) LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL — the readable form analysts often write
Return the user_id list sorted ascending. The expected count is small (1-5 users depending on the sample).

Schema:
```
viewing_activity(activity_id, user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS viewing_activity;
CREATE TABLE viewing_activity (
    activity_id    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id        INTEGER NOT NULL,
    content_name   TEXT,           -- nullable on purpose
    watch_date     DATE NOT NULL,
    watch_seconds  INTEGER NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO viewing_activity (user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds) VALUES
    -- u1 watched ST, never watched Witcher           → SHOULD appear
    (1, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-01', 2400),
    (1, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-02', 1800),
    (1, 'Black Mirror',       '2025-04-04', 2700),
    -- u2 watched ST AND Witcher                      → must NOT appear
    (2, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-01', 1500),
    (2, 'The Witcher',        '2025-04-03', 3000),
    -- u3 watched only Witcher                        → must NOT appear
    (3, 'The Witcher',        '2025-04-02', 1900),
    -- u4 watched ST, never Witcher                   → SHOULD appear
    (4, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-05', 2200),
    -- u5 watched neither                             → must NOT appear
    (5, 'Bridgerton',         '2025-04-06', 2000),
    -- The NULL that poisons NOT IN: a row from a 2-year-old data-quality
    -- incident where content_name failed to populate. Looks harmless.
    (6, NULL,                 '2024-01-15', 100);

-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SHAPE 1 — NOT IN  (THE TRAP)
--  Looks correct. Returns ZERO rows because the NOT IN
--  subquery contains a NULL, and `anything NOT IN (..., NULL)`
--  evaluates to UNKNOWN, which fails the WHERE filter.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SELECT DISTINCT user_id
--  FROM viewing_activity
--  WHERE content_name = 'Stranger Things'
--    AND user_id NOT IN (
--      SELECT user_id FROM viewing_activity
--      WHERE content_name = 'The Witcher'  -- but ONE row has content_name = NULL,
--                                          -- not joined here so it's fine HERE…
--    );
--  ↑ this specific subquery is OK because the inner WHERE excludes NULLs.
--  The trap fires when the inner subquery is broader, e.g.:
--    user_id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM other_table)
--  where other_table.user_id can be NULL.

-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SHAPE 2 — NOT EXISTS  (NULL-SAFE, USUALLY FASTEST)
--  Correlated anti-join. Returns u1 and u4.
--  Stops scanning the inner side as soon as one match is found.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WITH not_exists_answer AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT v1.user_id
    FROM viewing_activity v1
    WHERE v1.content_name = 'Stranger Things'
      AND NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1
        FROM viewing_activity v2
        WHERE v2.user_id = v1.user_id
          AND v2.content_name = 'The Witcher'
      )
),
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SHAPE 3 — LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL  (READABLE)
--  Same answer as NOT EXISTS. The planner usually rewrites
--  both to the same anti-join, but on older engines LEFT JOIN
--  can be slower because it materialises matched rows then
--  discards them.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
left_join_answer AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT v1.user_id
    FROM (SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM viewing_activity
          WHERE content_name = 'Stranger Things') v1
    LEFT JOIN viewing_activity v2
      ON v2.user_id = v1.user_id
     AND v2.content_name = 'The Witcher'
    WHERE v2.user_id IS NULL
)
SELECT user_id FROM not_exists_answer ORDER BY user_id;

questions.prompt:
Power-User Skew Analysis — P50 vs P99 + NTILE Decile Rollup + LAD-Tolerant View

Using viewing_activity, produce a per-decile rollup with (a) user_count, (b) average watch hours per user, (c) total watch hours, (d) the global skew_factor = P99 / P50 across all users. EXCLUDE users whose is_metadata_complete = false (LAD-tolerant view: a user whose newly-watched-show metadata hasn't propagated yet would otherwise get a temporarily-low watch count and land in the wrong decile). Sort decile ascending.

Then describe in a comment what the result tells the infra/marketing/content teams.

Schema:
```
viewing_activity(activity_id, user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS viewing_activity;
CREATE TABLE viewing_activity (
    activity_id           SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id               INTEGER NOT NULL,
    content_name          TEXT,
    watch_date            DATE NOT NULL,
    watch_seconds         INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_metadata_complete  BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Pareto-shaped sample: a handful of whales, a long tail of minnows.
INSERT INTO viewing_activity (user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete) VALUES
    -- Whale: user 1, 40 hours
    (1, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 36000, TRUE),
    -- Heavy: user 2, 20 hours
    (2, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (2, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    -- Heavy: user 3, 18 hours
    (3, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 32400, TRUE),
    (3, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 32400, TRUE),
    -- Mid: users 4-8 around 6-10 hours each
    (4, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (5, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 28800, TRUE),
    (6, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 25200, TRUE),
    (7, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 21600, TRUE),
    (8, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 21600, TRUE),
    -- Light: users 9-20 each ~2 hours
    (9,  'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 7200, TRUE),
    (10, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 7200, TRUE),
    (11, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 5400, TRUE),
    (12, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 5400, TRUE),
    (13, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 3600, TRUE),
    (14, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-01', 3600, TRUE),
    (15, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-02', 1800, TRUE),
    (16, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 1800, TRUE),
    (17, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-04', 1800, TRUE),
    (18, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-05', 900, TRUE),
    (19, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-01', 600, TRUE),
    (20, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-02', 300, TRUE),
    -- The LAD-incomplete user: would have been a whale, but their
    -- newly-watched-show metadata hasn't propagated yet. Excluding
    -- them avoids a false-positive low-decile assignment.
    (99, NULL,              '2025-04-05', 18000, FALSE);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  THE FULL POWER-USER ROLLUP                                    │
-- │   (a) per-decile user_count / avg / total                      │
-- │   (b) global skew_factor = P99 / P50                           │
-- │   (c) LAD-tolerant: is_metadata_complete = TRUE only           │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH user_metrics AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_seconds) / 3600.0 AS hours
    FROM viewing_activity
    WHERE is_metadata_complete = TRUE   -- LAD guard
    GROUP BY user_id
),
percentiles AS (
    SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)  AS median_hours,
           PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)  AS p99_hours
    FROM user_metrics
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT user_id, hours,
           NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) AS decile
    FROM user_metrics
)
SELECT
    decile,
    COUNT(*)                                                AS user_count,
    ROUND(AVG(hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                            AS avg_hours_per_user,
    ROUND(SUM(hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                            AS total_decile_hours,
    ROUND(((SELECT p99_hours FROM percentiles) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM percentiles), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS skew_factor_p99_over_p50
FROM ranked
GROUP BY decile
ORDER BY decile;

questions.prompt:
Whale / Dolphin / Minnow — Archetype Classification With Skewness Signals

Using viewing_activity, classify every user into one of four archetypes — whale (top 5%), heavy_dolphin (deciles 2-3), dolphin (deciles 4-8), minnow (bottom 20%). Exclude is_metadata_complete=FALSE rows from the metrics. Produce a per-archetype rollup with user_count, archetype share of total users, avg_hours, total_hours, plus the global skew_ratio (mean / median) and p99_over_p50 as constant columns. Sort whale first, minnow last.

Schema:
```
viewing_activity(activity_id, user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete)
```
questions.model_answer:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS viewing_activity;
CREATE TABLE viewing_activity (
    activity_id           SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id               INTEGER NOT NULL,
    content_name          TEXT,
    watch_date            DATE NOT NULL,
    watch_seconds         INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_metadata_complete  BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Pareto-shaped sample: one whale, two heavy dolphins, mid tier, long tail of minnows.
INSERT INTO viewing_activity (user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete) VALUES
    -- Whale (user 1): 40 hours across the week
    (1, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 36000, TRUE),
    -- Heavy dolphins (users 2, 3): 18-20 hours each
    (2, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (2, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (3, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 32400, TRUE),
    (3, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 32400, TRUE),
    -- Dolphins (users 4-13): 5-10 hours each
    (4, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (5, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 28800, TRUE),
    (6, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 25200, TRUE),
    (7, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 21600, TRUE),
    (8, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 21600, TRUE),
    (9,  'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-01', 18000, TRUE),
    (10, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-02', 18000, TRUE),
    (11, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-03', 14400, TRUE),
    (12, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-04', 14400, TRUE),
    (13, 'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-05', 10800, TRUE),
    -- Minnows (users 14-20): 0.5-3 hours each
    (14, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-01', 10800, TRUE),
    (15, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-02', 7200,  TRUE),
    (16, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-03', 5400,  TRUE),
    (17, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-04', 3600,  TRUE),
    (18, 'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-05', 1800,  TRUE),
    (19, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-01', 900,   TRUE),
    (20, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-02', 600,   TRUE),
    -- LAD-incomplete row — would otherwise look like a minnow (under-counted hours)
    -- and corrupt the deciles. The is_metadata_complete=FALSE filter excludes it.
    (99, NULL,             '2025-04-05', 18000, FALSE);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  ARCHETYPE CLASSIFICATION + SKEW SIGNALS                          │
-- │    • whale         = NTILE(20) bucket 1     (top 5%)              │
-- │    • heavy_dolphin = NTILE(10) buckets 2-3                        │
-- │    • dolphin       = NTILE(10) buckets 4-8                        │
-- │    • minnow        = NTILE(10) buckets 9-10 (bottom 20%)          │
-- │  Global stats:                                                    │
-- │    skew_ratio = AVG / MEDIAN  (1.0 = uniform; >2 = Pareto)       │
-- │    p99_over_p50 = 99th-pct / median  (interview-friendly variant) │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH user_metrics AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_seconds) / 3600.0 AS hours
    FROM viewing_activity
    WHERE is_metadata_complete = TRUE   -- LAD guard
    GROUP BY user_id
),
global_stats AS (
    SELECT
        AVG(hours)                                              AS mean_hours,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5)  WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)     AS median_hours,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)     AS p99_hours,
        COUNT(*)                                                AS total_users
    FROM user_metrics
),
classified AS (
    SELECT
        user_id, hours,
        CASE
            WHEN NTILE(20) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) = 1     THEN 'whale'
            WHEN NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) BETWEEN 2 AND 3 THEN 'heavy_dolphin'
            WHEN NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) BETWEEN 4 AND 8 THEN 'dolphin'
            ELSE                                                   'minnow'
        END AS archetype
    FROM user_metrics
)
SELECT
    c.archetype,
    COUNT(*)                                                AS user_count,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / (SELECT total_users FROM global_stats), 1)
                                                            AS pct_of_users,
    ROUND(AVG(c.hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                          AS avg_hours_per_user,
    ROUND(SUM(c.hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                          AS total_hours,
    ROUND(((SELECT mean_hours FROM global_stats) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM global_stats), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS skew_ratio_mean_over_median,
    ROUND(((SELECT p99_hours FROM global_stats) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM global_stats), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS p99_over_p50
FROM classified c
GROUP BY c.archetype
ORDER BY
    CASE c.archetype
        WHEN 'whale'         THEN 1
        WHEN 'heavy_dolphin' THEN 2
        WHEN 'dolphin'       THEN 3
        WHEN 'minnow'        THEN 4
    END;

questions.prompt:
Dynamic Pivot — The Question That Has No Clean Answer (JSON Aggregation + Three Workarounds)

Using employees(emp_id, name, salary, department), produce a 2-row result with the highest and second-highest salary per department. The catch: the department list isn't known upfront — adding 'Legal' tomorrow must NOT require a query rewrite. Use the schema-stable JSON-aggregation approach (jsonb_object_agg in Postgres / JSON_OBJECTAGG in MySQL 8 / OBJECT_AGG in Snowflake): the outer schema stays at 2 columns (salary_position, by_department), and the inner JSON object's keys evolve with the data.

Then in the enrichment compare with the other two approaches (two-step dynamic SQL, long-format + client pivot) and the production patterns (dbt get_column_values, schema-on-read, event-time dim registry).

Schema:
